structured_choices.data_source: EHR data from regional hospital network: 100,000 patient records spanning five years. Each record includes discharge encounter with linked 30-day post-discharge readmission status. Data extracted from production EHR systems with documented schema and data quality audits.
structured_choices.label_definition: Binary label: readmission = 1 if patient had an unplanned hospital readmission within 30 calendar days of discharge; readmission = 0 otherwise. Unplanned readmissions defined by admission type codes in the EHR (excluding planned procedures, transfers, or scheduled follow-up admissions). Label is determined at time of discharge and fixed retroactively—not subject to future changes.
structured_choices.split_method: Temporal split with stratification. Train: discharge dates from year 1–3 (approximately 60,000 records). Validation: discharge dates from year 4 (approximately 20,000 records). Test: discharge dates from year 5 (approximately 20,000 records). Within each split, stratify by readmission status to maintain class balance representation. Temporal split prevents data leakage and reflects deployment scenario where the model must generalize to future discharge cohorts. Stratification ensures the classifier is not biased by imbalanced classes within each fold.
structured_choices.preprocessing_fit_point: All preprocessing transformers (imputation, scaling, text tokenizers, vocabulary builders) are fit exclusively on the training set. The same transformers are applied (not re-fit) to validation and test sets. This prevents information from validation and test data influencing the learned transformations and avoids optimistic bias in performance estimates.
structured_choices.features: Two feature sets are constructed for fair comparison: (1) Baseline features: age, sex, comorbidity count (Charlson index), admission type, length of stay, primary diagnosis code (encoded as one-hot), insurance type, prior readmission history (count in previous 12 months). These are preprocessed via standard scaling (numerical) and one-hot encoding (categorical). (2) Transformer features: all discharge notes, medication lists, and clinical progress notes (free text) are tokenized and embedded using a pre-trained BioBERT model fine-tuned on clinical text. Text is truncated to 512 tokens. Both feature sets are aligned in temporal order: features capture only information available at discharge, with no information from the post-discharge period.
structured_choices.model: Transformer-based model: BioBERT encoder fine-tuned on the EHR text data, with a classification head (2-layer fully connected network with dropout) trained end-to-end on the readmission task. The model receives both embedded clinical text and baseline structured features (concatenated after embedding), output dimensionality 128. Trained with cross-entropy loss, batch size 32, Adam optimizer with learning rate 1e-4 (decayed), early stopping on validation AUC-ROC with patience of 5 epochs.
structured_choices.baseline: Logistic regression with L2 regularization trained on the baseline feature set (demographic + clinical features only). The baseline receives identical preprocessing (scaling, encoding, imputation) applied to the same train set. Hyperparameter tuning for baseline: grid search over regularization strength (C ∈ {0.001, 0.01, 0.1, 1, 10}) using 5-fold cross-validation on the training set, selecting C that maximizes validation AUC-ROC. This ensures the baseline is given equivalent tuning effort and is fairly compared on the same feature engineering protocol.
structured_choices.primary_metric: Area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUC-ROC). Justification: AUC-ROC measures the model's ability to rank-order patients by readmission risk across all decision thresholds, appropriate when the hospital has flexibility in how many flagged cases to review (different thresholds serve different resource constraints). AUC-ROC is threshold-agnostic and robust to class imbalance, making it a principled choice for this imbalanced problem. It aligns with the stakeholder's objective to identify high-risk patients reliably regardless of the operational threshold selected later.
secondary_metrics: Area under the precision-recall curve (AUPRC): because class imbalance is significant, AUPRC provides complementary insight into performance on the minority (readmitted) class.
Sensitivity and specificity at the operating point selected by the hospital (e.g., 10% false positive rate): operationally relevant for understanding trade-offs.
Calibration: expected calibration error (ECE) computed on test set to ensure predicted probabilities are reliable for risk stratification.
structured_choices.model_selection_data: Hyperparameter tuning and early stopping use the validation set (year 4 data) only. For the transformer model: validation AUC-ROC is computed every epoch; training stops when validation AUC-ROC does not improve for 5 consecutive epochs. No information from the test set influences model selection. The test set is held completely separate and used only for final performance reporting.
structured_choices.test_set_policy: Test set (year 5 discharge cohort) is locked and used exactly once, at the end of the experiment. No hyperparameter adjustments, retraining, or threshold tuning are performed after test set evaluation. Primary and secondary metrics are computed on the held-out test set and reported with 95% confidence intervals (bootstrap resampling, 1000 iterations).
structured_choices.confound_controls: Follow-up care and outpatient resources are documented in EHRs and used as covariates in a post-hoc stratified analysis: test set performance is stratified by hospital site (if applicable), insurance type, and prior outpatient visit frequency. This reveals whether the transformer's advantage holds consistently across patients with different access to follow-up care, or whether differences in follow-up practices confound the comparison. Any interaction effects (transformer advantage larger in high-resource settings) are reported. The design cannot eliminate confounding through randomization (observational data), but stratified analysis establishes the robustness of the finding and bounds the threat.

design_narrative:
## Experiment Design: Transformer-Based Readmission Prediction vs. Logistic Regression

### 1. Data Source and Label Definition

The experiment uses electronic health records from a regional hospital network covering 100,000 patient discharges over five years. The outcome label is binary: readmission = 1 if the patient was admitted to any hospital in the network for an unplanned reason within 30 calendar days of discharge; readmission = 0 otherwise. Planned readmissions (elective procedures, scheduled follow-up) are excluded by filtering on admission type codes. The label is determined at discharge and fixed retrospectively, ensuring no future information leaks into the prediction task.

### 2. Data Split Strategy

A temporal split is used to reflect the deployment scenario and prevent leakage. The training set comprises discharges from years 1–3 (~60,000 records), the validation set from year 4 (~20,000 records), and the test set from year 5 (~20,000 records). Within each split, data are stratified by readmission status to maintain class-balance representation and avoid split imbalance. Temporal ordering ensures the model generalizes to future cohorts and precludes the use of information from later time periods during training.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

Two feature sets are constructed for fair comparison:

**Baseline features** (logistic regression): age, sex, Charlson comorbidity index, admission type, length of stay, primary diagnosis (one-hot encoded), insurance type, and prior readmission count (12-month history). All preprocessing transformers (scaling for numerical features, one-hot encoding for categories, mean imputation for missing values) are fit on the training set only and applied identically to validation and test sets. This prevents leakage and ensures unbiased evaluation.

**Transformer features** (BioBERT model): all free-text clinical notes (discharge summaries, progress notes, medication lists) are tokenized and embedded using BioBERT, a pre-trained clinical transformer model. Text is truncated to 512 tokens to manage computation. The final embedding is concatenated with the baseline structured features. Both feature sets capture information available at discharge; no post-discharge outcomes or notes are included.

### 4. Model and Baseline

The **transformer model** is BioBERT fine-tuned end-to-end on the readmission classification task. It receives embedded clinical text and structured features (concatenated, embedded to 128 dimensions), followed by a 2-layer fully connected classification head with dropout. Training uses cross-entropy loss, Adam optimizer (learning rate 1e-4 with decay), batch size 32, and early stopping on validation AUC-ROC (patience 5 epochs).

The **baseline** is logistic regression with L2 regularization, trained on the baseline feature set only. This baseline is deliberately strong: it receives the same preprocessing protocol and hyperparameter tuning effort as the transformer. Specifically, regularization strength (C) is tuned via grid search over {0.001, 0.01, 0.1, 1, 10} using 5-fold cross-validation on the training set, selecting C to maximize validation AUC-ROC. This ensures the comparison is fair and isolates the contribution of the transformer's architecture and text representations.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric**: Area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUC-ROC). This metric is appropriate because (a) the hospital has flexibility in selecting an operating threshold for risk flagging, (b) AUC-ROC is robust to class imbalance and threshold-agnostic, and (c) it directly measures the model's ability to rank-order patients by readmission risk, aligning with the operational objective of identifying high-risk patients.

**Secondary metrics**: (1) Area under the precision-recall curve (AUPRC) to focus on performance on the minority readmitted class. (2) Sensitivity and specificity at a clinically relevant operating point (e.g., 10% false positive rate). (3) Expected calibration error (ECE) to ensure predicted probabilities can be reliably used for risk stratification.

### 6. Model Selection and Validation

Hyperparameter tuning and model selection use only the validation set (year 4). The transformer's early stopping criterion is validation AUC-ROC (no improvement for 5 consecutive epochs). For the baseline, the optimal C is selected from the grid search based on validation AUC-ROC from 5-fold cross-validation on the training set. No information from the test set is used for tuning.

The test set (year 5 discharge cohort) is held locked and used exactly once at the end of the experiment. All primary and secondary metrics are computed on the test set, and 95% confidence intervals are derived via bootstrap resampling (1000 iterations).

### 7. Controls for Confounds

Differences in outpatient care resources and follow-up practices could confound the observed readmission rates. Post-hoc stratified analysis reveals test-set performance broken down by site, insurance type, and outpatient visit frequency. If the transformer's advantage is consistent across these strata, the finding is robust; if the advantage disappears in low-resource settings, this signals a confound interaction. This stratification does not eliminate confounding but establishes the generalizability and boundary conditions of the result.

### 8. Success Criterion

The hypothesis is supported if the transformer model achieves a statistically significantly higher test-set AUC-ROC than the baseline logistic regression (two-tailed test, α = 0.05, with 95% confidence intervals not overlapping or p-value < 0.05 from a bootstrap difference test). Secondary metrics are examined for consistency; if AUPRC or calibration favors one model, these inform interpretation but do not override the primary metric.
